tossups.question: A poem about this event describes the sun as it “goeth” “under wood” and “under tree.” In a depiction of this event, a woman sees a “little thing the size of a hazelnut,” which represents “all that is made.” In the earliest known English-language book written by a woman, the author describes the “showings” of this event that appeared to her after a near-death experience. The first printed version of a poem about this event antedates a version carved onto a (*) stone monument in Ruthwell. In a poem about the “best of dreams” collected in the Vercelli Codex, a jewel-encrusted object that was central to this event recounts the story of its own creation. For 10 points, name this Biblical event described in the “Dream of the Rood,” when “all creation wept, bewailed the King’s fall.”
tossups.question_sanitized: A poem about this event describes the sun as it "goeth" "under wood" and "under tree." In a depiction of this event, a woman sees a "little thing the size of a hazelnut," which represents "all that is made." In the earliest known English-language book written by a woman, the author describes the "showings" of this event that appeared to her after a near-death experience. The first printed version of a poem about this event antedates a version carved onto a (*) stone monument in Ruthwell. In a poem about the "best of dreams" collected in the Vercelli Codex, a jewel-encrusted object that was central to this event recounts the story of its own creation. For 10 points, name this Biblical event described in the "Dream of the Rood," when "all creation wept, bewailed the King's fall."
tossups.answer: the Crucifixion of Jesus [accept the death of Christ or descriptions of Jesus on the Cross]
tossups.answer_sanitized: the Crucifixion of Jesus [accept the death of Christ or descriptions of Jesus on the Cross]

tossups.question: In a poem sometimes titled for one of these objects, a character has his name changed after helping the protagonist dig up three of these objects. Edward Pettit’s The Waning Sword compares the title object to ice, a candle, and one of these objects. Another poem claims that, “Hope was renewed / with cheer and bliss for those who were burning” after an action is performed with one of these objects. That poem is about an object that begins bleeding on the right side and is (*) bedecked with gems and gold. Along with Cynewulf’s Elene, that poem about a tree that becomes one of these objects appears in the Vercelli Codex. For 10 points, The Dream of the Rood is about what type of object, central to Christian imagery?
tossups.question_sanitized: In a poem sometimes titled for one of these objects, a character has his name changed after helping the protagonist dig up three of these objects. Edward Pettit's The Waning Sword compares the title object to ice, a candle, and one of these objects. Another poem claims that, "Hope was renewed / with cheer and bliss for those who were burning" after an action is performed with one of these objects. That poem is about an object that begins bleeding on the right side and is (*) bedecked with gems and gold. Along with Cynewulf's Elene, that poem about a tree that becomes one of these objects appears in the Vercelli Codex. For 10 points, The Dream of the Rood is about what type of object, central to Christian imagery?

tossups.question: In one poem in a genre named for this phenomenon, the poet misunderstands a metaphor and tells the black knight not to worry about a game of chess, but the knight is trying to tell him that his beloved White is dead. In that poem, the corpse of Ceyx speaks to Alycone after being brought this phenomenon. A genre of poetry named for this phenomenon includes both The Book of the Duchess and The Legend of Good Women. A poem titled for this phenomenon from the (*) Vercelli Codex includes an inanimate wooden object which praises God after narrating how it was pierced with nails. Will meets Reason, Truth and Conscience whilst experiencing this phenomenon in Piers Plowman. For 10 points, a medieval poem which details the crucifixion from the perspective of the cross is titled for which phenomenon of the Rood?
tossups.question_sanitized: In one poem in a genre named for this phenomenon, the poet misunderstands a metaphor and tells the black knight not to worry about a game of chess, but the knight is trying to tell him that his beloved White is dead. In that poem, the corpse of Ceyx speaks to Alycone after being brought this phenomenon. A genre of poetry named for this phenomenon includes both The Book of the Duchess and The Legend of Good Women. A poem titled for this phenomenon from the (*) Vercelli Codex includes an inanimate wooden object which praises God after narrating how it was pierced with nails. Will meets Reason, Truth and Conscience whilst experiencing this phenomenon in Piers Plowman. For 10 points, a medieval poem which details the crucifixion from the perspective of the cross is titled for which phenomenon of the Rood?

bonuses.leadin: In the poem Elene (eh-LAY-nuh), a character is renamed Cyriacus after finding three of these objects underground. For 10 points each:
parts: Name these objects, which proclaim "I appear iridescent; my form is shining now" in a riddle by Tatwine (TAT-ween-uh). One of these objects narrates the second section of a poem found in the Vercelli Codex and usually attributed to Caedmon (KAD-mun) or Cynewulf (KIN-ee-wulf).
This Old English poem is preserved in the Vercelli Codex. This poem imagines the story of the crucifixion from the perspective of the cross, which is covered in gems.
The Dream of the Rood uses this device to underpin its poetic meter. Beowulf also uses this device, which forms patterns using syllables starting with similar sounds.
answers: crosses [or crucifixes or the True Cross; accept, but do NOT reveal, roods or rōd or rōda]
answers_sanitized: crosses [or crucifixes or the True Cross; accept, but do NOT reveal, roods or rod or roda]
alliteration [or alliterative verse; prompt on assonance or consonance]

This Old English poem is preserved in the Vercelli Codex. This poem imagines the story of the crucifixion from the perspective of the cross, which is covered in gems.
The Dream of the Rood uses this device to underpin its poetic meter. Beowulf also uses this device, which forms patterns using syllables starting with similar sounds.

bonuses.leadin: Variously attributed to Caedmon and Cynewulf, this poem is primarily preserved in the Vercelli Codex, and is thought to be one of the oldest surviving in the Old English corpus. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this poem. Its first section describes the narrator’s vision of the Cross, while its second part describes Christ’s crucifixion from the perspective of the cross itself.

Predating any Old English manuscripts, this large eighth-century stone cross, named for the Scottish border town where it still stands, is believed to independently preserve part of The Dream of the Rood.
The attribution of the poem to Caedmon is based primarily on circumstantial evidence in the Ecclesiastical History of the English People, a work by this “venerable” monk of Jarrow.
